content:
Lets talk loras

What if i wanted to make a custom lora, for a tiny local model (likely grmma4 a4b)
To fit for exactly one rpg campaign, style and flavor.

How hard would this be? How big dataset etc would i need? And how to build that?

{"query":"Gemma 4 A4B model specs MoE active parameters"}
{"url":"https://unsloth.ai/docs/models/gemma-4"}
Short version: the *mechanics* are easy now — Unsloth has first-class Gemma 4 fine-tuning support and a style LoRA is a few-hours job once the data's ready. The hard part is entirely the dataset, plus one hardware gotcha that bites people.

**The hardware gotcha first.** You run 26B-A4B comfortably because at inference only ~4B of the 26B params are active and you've got weights spilling into your 128 GB of RAM. Training doesn't work that way — all 26 billion parameters must be loaded into memory regardless of routing, so QLoRA has to hold the full 4-bit base (~14–15 GB) plus gradients and activations. That won't fit in the 4070's 12 GB. Three ways around it:

- **Train E4B (dense ~4–5B) instead** — fits in 12 GB with headroom, and is arguably the more honest "tiny model" target anyway.
- **Rent a 24 GB+ cloud GPU** (4090/L40S) for an hour. A style LoRA is cheap enough that this is genuinely ~$1–3.
- **Unsloth CPU offload** — possible on 12 GB but slow and fiddly; only worth it if you're allergic to cloud.

**Dataset — smaller than you'd think, fussier than you'd think.** Style/flavor is one of the cheapest things to teach: a few hundred to ~1–2k good examples is plenty, and overfeeding *hurts* (tiny corpus + too many epochs = it memorizes your old scenes instead of learning the voice). And you already have the corpus — sessions in the 60s for The Long Watch alone is a goldmine, and your export pipeline already turns transcripts into structured outputs. Extend it to emit JSONL.

Two rules that make or break it:
- **Train on GM turns only.** Mask the loss so it learns from your narration, not the player inputs (Unsloth's `train_on_responses_only`).
- **Diversity within the voice.** Combat, quiet dialogue, introspection, scene-setting all represented — otherwise it learns "this scene," not "this register."

Each example is just: chat context (system preamble + prior turns) → the GM response as target. Your transcripts, sliced.

**The build.** Export → JSONL (system / user=player / model=GM, loss-masked to GM turns) → Unsloth notebook or `unsloth studio` (local no-code UI), pick the Gemma 4 base. For style, sane defaults are r=16–32, alpha = r or 2r, 1–3 epochs, LR ~2e-4, target all-linear. Watch a held-out scene each epoch; stop when the voice locks in but general coherence is still intact. Then export to GGUF.

**The part that fits your life:** llama.cpp loads LoRA adapters at runtime (`--lora`) without merging. So you don't bake a franken-model — you keep one Gemma 4 base and hot-swap a per-campaign adapter: one for The Long Watch's register, one for Solramis's cold-warmth, one for Rust and Ruin. Given how many campaigns you juggle, that's the pattern I'd build toward.

**One gut-check.** For *pure* flavor, ask whether your GM userstyle (you're on 5.5) plus a few-shot anchor isn't already 85% of the way there. A LoRA earns its keep when you want a *small* model to hold a register it can't manage zero-shot — which is exactly why the real payoff here is making E4B punch above its weight for fast local sessions, more than retuning 26B-A4B.

Want me to draft the JSONL schema and the export-pipeline extension, or the actual Unsloth training script?

Curating would be a pain. Yes, i habe almost 70 sessions of that campaign, yes. But alot of it is...imperfect, the whole reason we want to unify the style

(Also, for curiousity, you say 1 hour of renting 24gb compute for grmma4. What'd that number be for...opus? :) Quick napkin math only as thats a scenario thatll likely never happen :p )

Then the imperfect-data point flips the whole approach — for the better, actually.

If the sessions are inconsistent and unifying them is the *goal*, training on them raw is self-defeating: you'd be teaching the model the exact inconsistency you're trying to escape. Naive imitation can't bootstrap a voice the data doesn't already hold.

So don't curate — **distill**. Your userstyle 5.5 is already a written spec for the canonical voice. Hand a strong model (Opus, ironically) a raw scene plus that spec and have it *rewrite* the scene into the unified voice. The rewrite is your training pair. Now the dataset is consistent by construction, and "curation" collapses into a glance-and-discard pass on the rewrites that drifted — automatable, drops straight into your export pipeline, a fraction of the manual pain.

What you get is a small model that learned "Opus performing my style," compressed into a LoRA. Gemma won't inherit the reasoning, but cadence/voice/flavor is exactly the surface layer that distills cleanly — which is the entire job of a style LoRA. Nice side effect: seed the rewrites from your *best* moments and let Opus normalize the rest toward them, so the unified voice is anchored in scenes you already loved rather than an abstract spec.

**The Opus napkin** 😄

Can't, of course — Opus weights live inside Anthropic, there's no rent-a-box-and-LoRA-it path. But as pure scale fantasy:

Gemma 26B-A4B fits one 24 GB card at 4-bit → ~$2 and an hour. Opus's size is undisclosed, but frontier-class means it doesn't fit *a* GPU — even a LoRA has to hold the frozen base resident for every forward/backward pass, so you're looking at a multi-node H100/H200 cluster just to *load* it. Call it somewhere between 8 and 64+ 80 GB cards at ~$2–4/hr each → order of $100–1,000+ per run, multiple hours, before you've touched a hyperparameter.

And that's the cheap part. The LoRA is a rounding error; the base model under it is the iceberg — pretraining something that size is tens of millions of dollars of compute. So your Gemma LoRA is a fun weekend. The Opus equivalent is "lease a datacenter slice."

So... 1-2k good examples..tricky, can maybe find 50 quality anchors to compare with. Maybe

Hundreds of 'decent'. Thousands...harder, will have to cull looser.

And... I'm abit lost on what criterias to cull at :p

And would need a framework for this

First, breathe on the numbers: you need far fewer than you're dreading. The 1–2k was my upper bound for raw imitation. With distillation and a tight voice, a few hundred *diverse* pairs is plenty — and a few hundred diverse beats a few thousand redundant, every time. Your 50 anchors plus the "hundreds of decent" is very likely the whole dataset. You probably never touch the thousands.

Now the reframe that answers "cull on what?" — with the rewrite approach you're filtering **twice, on two different criteria**, and conflating them is what's making it feel impossible.

**Filter 1 — raw scenes going *into* the rewrite.** These don't need to be good *prose*; Opus fixes that. You select on *situation*, not polish:
- **Voice-bearing?** Actual narration here, or just mechanical bookkeeping (rolls, inventory, "you take 4 damage")? Drop the transactional stuff.
- **Rewritable?** Is what-happened clear enough that a rewrite would be *normalization*, not *invention*? If even you can't tell what the good version is, Opus will hallucinate one. Drop.
- **New?** Covers a beat you don't already have five of? If it's the eighth tavern-haggle, skip it — redundancy doesn't teach voice, it overfits situations.

**Filter 2 — the post-rewrite QC gate.** *This* is where strictness lives, and it's cheap: read Opus's output, kill anything that drifted off-voice, invented plot, or went incoherent. Ten-second judgments, not craft evaluations.

So your 50 anchors aren't training data — they're the **style bible** Opus reads to know the target. Pick them to *span the range* (combat / dread / tenderness / quiet / exposition), not "top 50 by quality." Fifty great combat scenes teach a lopsided voice.

The framework, then, is just a tagging pass: each scene → `{register, voice-bearing y/n, rewritable y/n, redundant y/n, anchor-grade y/n}`. And you don't hand-label that — it's a classifier prompt, one more stage in the pipeline you already run. You spot-check its calls; it does the grind.

Best part: don't assemble the perfect set upfront. Train on ~300, see *which registers it flubs*, add data only there. Tight loop, patch the gap — much more your speed than front-loading a 2k spreadsheet.

Want me to write the actual rubric plus the scene-scoring prompt as a pipeline stage? That's the operational version of all this.

I probably have at least 5-10 *different* styles of combat to curate for different types of scenes actually 
And...less of a clue for what kindof scenes to look for for the rest.
For the long watch, jump travel, starship stuff, etc. That often ends up wrong in the model (even opus ends up putting sails on starship at times...)

Ah — this is two different problems wearing one coat, and pulling them apart is the unlock.

**Voice** (cadence, beat structure, tone) is what we've been curating for. But "sails on starships" isn't a voice failure — it's a **setting-fidelity** failure. The model knows *how* to write; it just grabs the wrong furniture, because its prior drags "ship + travel" toward age-of-sail before it reaches your canon. That's a *default-association* problem, not a prose problem.

And the good news: shifting defaults is something a LoRA does *better* than voice. Memorizing facts, no — but retraining "in this world, ships move like X, not like sailing" is exactly reshaping the probability mass, which is what the adapter is for. So your jump/starship register is a legit, high-value target — arguably more valuable than the voice work, because it fixes the thing prompting keeps failing to suppress.

It does change how you build that slice, though:

- It has to be **contrastive** — heavy on the exact scenes where the cliché *would* fire (jumps, dockings, transit, ship-as-place) rendered in your correct idiom. You're not just showing good scenes, you're showing the model the fork and which way to take it.
- The rewrite step needs **guardrails** here or it betrays you. You already caught Opus sailing-on-starships — so a blind rewrite will *launder the cliché straight into your training data*. For this register the rewrite prompt has to carry the canon plus an explicit banned-metaphor list, and the post-rewrite QC checks one thing above all: did it sail-on-starship again?

Which makes your anchors for this register especially load-bearing — they're the counter-cliché reference Opus reads to *not* drift.

Now, "less of a clue what to look for for the rest" — don't enumerate, **discover**. You won't name all the registers up front; you'd miss the ones you can't see. Run a pass that tags each scene with a freeform "what kind of scene is this," then cluster the tags. The taxonomy falls out of your actual play. Combat self-splits into its real 5–10 sub-types instead of you pre-naming them, and the non-combat categories you're fuzzy on just *appear*. Then you name the clusters and curate to cover each — watching especially for clusters that come back cliché-dominated. Those are your "sails" hotspots.

One line worth drawing: don't ask the LoRA to *memorize* hard canon. It shifts idiom and defaults beautifully but is a blunt instrument for exact facts — drive numbers, named tech, who's who. That's lorebook/worldinfo territory, which you already run in SillyTavern. So the clean split is **LoRA for voice + setting-*idiom*, lorebook for the hard facts**. Overload the adapter with physics and it'll memorize them sloppily and forget half; pin the physics in worldinfo and let the LoRA handle "this world *feels* like this."
